allergens: gluten (from soy sauce and rice wine)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Guangxi Black Vinegar Spare Ribs is approximately 250 g, comprising 4–6 bone-in pork rib pieces glazed in a dark, tangy black vinegar sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Pork, fresh, loin, blade (chops), bone-in, cooked, braised — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; adapted with black vinegar sauce component estimated from USDA condiment data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Guangxi Black Vinegar Spare Ribs exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for braised pork spare ribs (NDB No. 10094, approximately 297 kcal/100g, protein 25.6g, fat 21.0g, carbohydrate 0g) with an estimated sauce contribution (black vinegar, soy sauce, sugar) adding approximately 15–20 kcal and 5–8g carbohydrate per 100g of finished dish. Final per-100g values represent the composite dish including sauce glaze. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — meat and mixed protein-fat dishes; analogous estimate based on braised pork with sweet-sour sauce
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Guangxi Black Vinegar Spare Ribs. GI estimated at 35 based on analogous braised pork dishes with sweet-sour sauce components from the University of Sydney GI Database. High protein and fat content significantly blunts glycemic response. The added sugar and black vinegar sauce contribute moderate carbohydrate (7.8g/100g), but the overall GI remains low due to the dominant protein-fat matrix. GL per serving = (35 × 7.8 × 250) / (100 × 100) = 6.8.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg/100g; ~1200 mg per serving) from soy sauce, dark soy sauce, and black vinegar
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a standard serving and request reduced-soy-sauce preparation, as a full 250g serving provides approximately 1200 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate added sugar from black vinegar glaze (~5.2g sugar/100g; ~13g per serving) combined with low GI of 35
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low glycemic index (estimated 35) and high protein content make this dish relatively manageable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, but the added sugar in the black vinegar glaze (~13g per serving) should be accounted for in daily carbohydrate budgeting, and portion control is advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~4.3g/100g; ~10.8g per serving) and high sodium from soy-based sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content from pork spare ribs (~10.8g per 250g serving) and high sodium load (~1200 mg per serving) are both relevant cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional small portions and choose leaner cuts when possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Guangxi Black Vinegar Spare Ribs (广西黑醋小排) is a classic braised pork rib dish originating from Guangxi province in southern China, widely served in Chinese restaurants across Singapore and Southeast Asia. The dish features bone-in pork spare ribs that are typically marinated, briefly deep-fried to seal in juices, and then slowly braised in a rich sauce made from Chinese black vinegar (镇江香醋 or similar dark rice vinegar), soy sauce, dark soy sauce, sugar, garlic, ginger, and Shaoxing rice wine. The result is tender, fall-off-the-bone pork coated in a glossy, dark, sweet-tangy glaze with a complex umami depth. The black vinegar imparts a distinctive mellow sourness that balances the sweetness of the sugar and the savouriness of the soy, creating a flavour profile that is simultaneously sweet, sour, salty, and rich. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-to-high calorie protein source (~215 kcal/100g, ~18.5g protein/100g) with significant fat content from the pork (~12.5g fat/100g, ~4.3g saturated fat/100g). The sauce contributes meaningful sodium (~480 mg/100g) and added sugar (~5.2g/100g), making sodium and sugar management relevant for individuals with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 35 (low), as the dominant protein-fat matrix blunts glycemic response despite the sweet glaze. A typical restaurant serving is approximately 250g. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding gluten (soy sauce) or sesame. It is a popular sharing dish in Cantonese-influenced Chinese restaurants and is commonly ordered as part of a multi-dish family-style meal.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Guangxi Black Vinegar Spare Ribs exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy braised pork spare rib data with an estimated sauce contribution. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on comparable braised pork dishes. Medium confidence reflects the estimation approach; values are nutritionally defensible but not directly measured.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated composites: USDA SR Legacy braised pork spare rib base (NDB No. 10094) plus estimated black vinegar sauce contribution (black vinegar, soy sauce, sugar, garlic, ginger). No direct laboratory analysis or approved-source entry for this specific dish was found. 2. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 3. Sodium value (~480 mg/100g) is a key clinical concern and is based on estimated soy sauce and black vinegar contribution; actual restaurant sodium may vary significantly. 4. The dish is not halal-certified as it contains pork. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy pork spare rib data and are not directly measured for this dish; these values should be treated as indicative only. 6. No sentinel values (-1) are used in this record; all micronutrient fields have been populated with USDA-derived estimates rather than left as unknown, as the USDA SR Legacy data provides a reasonable compositional basis for the pork component.
